text.title: Linear Agent as First-Class Assignee Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the tracker-linear-agent-as-assignee methodology plus the `skip-this-methodology` safety rule. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Every coding agent MUST have its own Linear user account (`agent-cursor`, `agent-devin`, etc.), not share a single `bot` account. Avatar and display name reflect the underlying vendor.

rationale.source: Linear MCP docs (2026) + Internal — identity policy

rule.rationale: Shared bot accounts collapse the audit trail — every action looks the same; per-vendor accounts let reviewers see which agent did what.

rule.statement: When an issue is assigned to an agent identity, the agent MUST within 60 seconds post a task list, an ETA, and switch the issue to `In Progress`. Silent agents are non-compliant.
rule.rationale: An assignment with no contract reply makes the human wonder if the agent saw the work; the contract closes that uncertainty in one comment.

rule.statement: While work is active the agent MUST post a checkpoint comment every 10-15 minutes including elapsed time + current step. No-progress windows ≥ 30 min require a `blocked:` comment.
rule.rationale: Long silences erode trust; checkpoints give the human a permanent feel for whether the agent is making progress or stuck.

rule.statement: On completion the agent MUST post a final comment containing the PR link + a one-line summary, then transition the issue to `In Review`. The agent does NOT transition to `Done`.
rule.rationale: Done is the human owner's transition (after merge); agents stop at In Review so humans retain control of closure.

rule.statement: Every agent action on a Linear issue MUST appear as a comment, status change, or PR link tied to an assignment event. Mutating the issue without a visible record is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Silent side-effects (label changes, hidden field updates) make the agent's behavior unaccountable.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the preconditions in `## Applies If` are not all true, or any item in `## Skip If` holds. The decision tree routes to this rule on those branches.
rule.rationale: A methodology that runs on the wrong input wastes tokens and produces misleading artifacts. The explicit skip rule is the safety valve.
